summary: Monday was a big day for Reynoldstown with a long-stalled Beltline development finally breaking ground, while ICE agents showed up at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with massive TSA lines. Plus Inman Park just got a hot new Asian fusion spot called Mushi Ni.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Stalled Beltline Development Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting as an empty lot for nearly five years, construction has finally started on a mixed-use project at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace. The 85-unit development is just a block from the Beltline's Eastside Trail and will include both townhomes and condos. Developer Embry took over the project from national builder Toll Brothers two years ago.
topStory.body:
You know that empty corner lot near the Beltline in Reynoldstown that's been sitting there forever? Well, it's finally happening. Construction crews are now working on what will become 85 new residential units at 195 Chester Avenue, just a block west of the Eastside Trail where everyone jogs and walks their dogs.

The project, now called Camber Crossing, has been in limbo since Toll Brothers cleared the site back in 2021. Atlanta-based Embry Development stepped in two years ago and took over the permitted plans, which call for a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos. If you're familiar with the area, this is right where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace, about a block north of Memorial Drive.

This is exactly the kind of development Reynoldstown neighbors have been watching closely as the area continues to evolve with Beltline proximity. The fact that these will be for-sale units rather than just more rentals is noteworthy too, giving people a chance to actually own something in this increasingly hot neighborhood.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at 195 Chester Avenue in Reynoldstown showing early development work

The project, now called Camber Crossing, has been in limbo since Toll Brothers cleared the site back in 2021. stories.headline: Decatur Gets Village-Style 'Missing Middle' Housing for Under $700K
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway will bring 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. With Decatur home prices hitting over $700K median, this project aims to offer more accessible single-family options ITP. The pocket neighborhood will replace two underused lots totaling 2.1 acres.
stories.body:
If you've been priced out of Decatur proper (and honestly, who hasn't with median home values over $700K), there's some hope on the horizon. Cottages at Midway is starting to take shape on Midway Road, and it's designed as exactly the kind of "missing middle" housing we need more of around here.

The development calls for 18 standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards and green space, which sounds a lot more appealing than another massive apartment complex. These will be for-sale homes, not rentals, and while they haven't announced pricing yet, the whole point is to be more accessible than what's currently on the market in Decatur.

What I like about this is the village concept. Instead of just cramming as many units as possible onto the 2.1-acre site, they're actually thinking about community space and how people interact with their neighbors. It's the kind of thoughtful infill development that could be a model for other ITP neighborhoods dealing with the same affordability crunch.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway showing village-style housing layout

stories.headline: West End's Rare For-Sale Townhomes Start Taking Shape Along Beltline
stories.summary: GW Parkside is finally rising at 600 Hopkins Street, where developer Ackerman & Co. (the Lee + White folks) cleared the site a year ago. The 23-unit project will be some of the few for-sale townhomes along the Southwest Beltline corridor. First units should hit the market this spring.
stories.body:
Remember when Ackerman & Co. cleared that site on Hopkins Street in the West End last year? Well, now we can finally see what they're building there, and it's looking pretty good. GW Parkside (formerly called Hopkins and White Townhomes) is starting to take shape, and these will be some of the rare for-sale townhomes you can actually buy along this stretch of the Beltline.

Ackerman knows what they're doing in this area. They're the same developer behind Lee + White, that former warehouse complex that's become such a food and office destination nearby. So they understand the neighborhood and what works here. The first seven of the eventual 23 units are nearly topped out, and marketing materials suggest they'll start selling this spring.

What's significant about this project is that there just aren't many opportunities to buy (versus rent) new construction townhomes along the Southwest Beltline. Most of what gets built is apartments or condos. For people who want to live near the trail but also want a little outdoor space and the feeling of a house, this could be exactly what they've been waiting for.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Fusion Magic to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chef power couple Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect elevated bao like soft shell crab and chicken longanisa, plus Peking chicken platters and Thai tea martinis. Both chefs bring serious credentials from Gordon Ramsay and other top kitchens.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a serious dining upgrade with the opening of Mushi Ni on Elizabeth Street. This is the first restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, and if you know anything about their backgrounds, you know this place is going to be special. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, so the pedigree is there.

The menu is Asian-American comfort food done right. We're talking elevated bao like soft shell crab and chicken longanisa, plus shareable plates of umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs. The Peking chicken platter feeds two people for $25, which feels reasonable for this level of cooking. And yes, they have Thai tea martinis, because sometimes you need a cocktail with your bao.

The location is perfect too, right off the Freedom Park Trail where people are always walking and biking. It's the kind of neighborhood spot that could become a regular hang, especially if the food lives up to the early buzz. Given that this started as a successful pop-up concept, they've clearly figured out what works.
stories.imageAlt: Interior of Mushi Ni restaurant showing modern Asian fusion dining space

stories.headline: Gansevoort Market Creator Opens Food Hall Near East Lake
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, who created NYC's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works in mid-April. The food hall will anchor the new Vela Park apartment community near East Lake Golf Club with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. This is Montwaid's third Atlanta food destination.
stories.body:
The guy who created New York's Gansevoort Market is bringing his food hall magic to the eastside. Robert Montwaid's Vela Park Food Works is set to open in mid-April as the centerpiece of the new Vela Park apartment community near East Lake Golf Club, and if his track record means anything, this is going to be worth the trip.

The concept follows Montwaid's formula of bringing together multiple chef-driven vendors under one roof, but this isn't just a grab-and-go situation. They're planning six different concepts with craft cocktails and serious outdoor dining space. Given that this is anchoring a whole new residential development, they're clearly betting people will make this a regular destination, not just a one-time visit.

What's interesting is how this area near East Lake is quietly becoming a food destination. You've got the golf course, you've got good transit access, and now you'll have this food hall. It's the kind of project that could really change how people think about this part of town, especially if Montwaid brings the same level of vendor curation that made his other Atlanta projects successful.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing modern food hall design

stories.headline: Austin's Lewis Barbecue Lands at Ansley Mall with Epic Brisket
stories.summary: The pitmaster behind some of Austin's best barbecue just opened behind The Cook's Warehouse at Ansley Mall. John Lewis built custom 22-foot smokers that run 24/7 in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the Beltline bridge. The brisket might be Atlanta's best new barbecue arrival.
stories.body:
Barbecue nerds, this one's for you. John Lewis, the pitmaster who helped put Austin barbecue on the map, just opened his smokehouse at Ansley Mall, and the setup alone is worth seeing. He's got six custom 22-foot smokers running 24 hours a day in a loft space with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks more like a fancy engine room than a typical barbecue joint.

Lewis welded these smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they're staggered in eight-hour shifts to keep the meat flowing. The result is some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta, with that perfect smoke ring and the kind of bark that makes you understand why people obsess over Texas barbecue. The loaded platters balance smoke and heat with just the right amount of sides.

The location behind The Cook's Warehouse is perfect for grabbing lunch before or after hitting the Beltline, and there's something poetic about watching all that smoke billowing while people walk and bike past on the trail below. This isn't just another barbecue place, this is a destination.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: ICE Agents Show Up at Hartsfield-Jackson But Don't Do Much
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are now stationed at the airport to help with massive TSA lines during the government shutdown, but travelers say they're mostly just standing around. With 41% of TSA agents calling out sick, wait times hit four hours on Monday. The ICE agents aren't doing security screening or immigration enforcement.
stories.body:
So those ICE agents that got deployed to Hartsfield-Jackson? Turns out they're not really doing much of anything useful. While TSA lines stretched for hours on Monday (we're talking four-hour waits), travelers noticed the immigration agents mostly standing around in tactical gear looking official but not actually helping move the security lines.

The partial government shutdown has TSA agents working without pay for over a month now, and 41% of Atlanta's workforce called out on Sunday alone. That's why you're seeing those insane lines that snake through the terminals. ICE agents were supposedly sent in to help ease congestion, but since they can't actually do security screening and they're not doing immigration enforcement either, it's unclear what exactly they're contributing.

Mayor Andre Dickens tried to reassure everyone that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, but honestly, if you're stuck in a four-hour security line, you're probably more worried about missing your flight than anything else. The real solution here is paying TSA agents so they actually show up to work.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents walking through Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Is Almost Done
stories.summary: West Howard Avenue between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA is getting the full treatment with bike lanes, better sidewalks, and traffic calming. The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers and connects Decatur High School to the East Lake station. It's been in the works for nearly a decade.
stories.body:
After nearly 10 years of planning, Decatur's big Complete Streets project on West Howard Avenue is finally wrapping up, and it's looking pretty good. The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High) to the city limits at East Lake MARTA station now has proper bike infrastructure, wider sidewalks, and traffic calming features that should make this corridor actually pleasant to navigate on foot or bike.

The centerpiece is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers that runs most of the length of the project. This isn't just painted bike lanes that drivers ignore, these are physically protected bike lanes that give cyclists a real sense of safety. The timing makes sense too, connecting Decatur High School students to transit and giving people a safe way to bike between downtown Decatur and the East Lake area.

This is exactly the kind of infrastructure investment that other cities should be copying. Instead of just moving cars as fast as possible, Decatur actually thought about how people want to get around without a car. The fact that it took nearly a decade to happen shows how hard it is to change street design, but the result is worth the wait.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls as Fire Union Fight Heats Up
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and require documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of residents showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' first-ever union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is moving forward with some serious pushback against federal immigration enforcement, unanimously advancing two resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council votes on April 20, and this feels like the kind of thing that's going to pass easily.

But the bigger drama at the Public Safety Committee meeting was about firefighters. Dozens of people showed up to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract, even though council ratified it last year. That's some serious political pressure, especially with everything else going on right now.

The ICE resolutions are interesting timing, given that immigration agents are literally walking around Hartsfield-Jackson right now supposedly helping with TSA lines. Atlanta has always been a more immigrant-friendly city than the rest of Georgia, so this feels like local officials trying to stake out their position before federal policy gets more aggressive.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking about ICE and fire union issues

stories.headline: Park Pride Conference Tackles Climate Resilience at Botanical Garden
stories.summary: Hundreds of park advocates from across the Southeast gathered for the 25th annual Parks & Greenspace Conference with a focus on "Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities." The event highlighted how urban heat islands disproportionately affect underserved Atlanta neighborhoods and what communities can do about it.
stories.body:
The Southeast's biggest annual parks conference happened right here in Atlanta at the Botanical Garden, and this year's theme of "Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities" couldn't be more relevant. With hundreds of attendees from as far as Massachusetts and Minnesota, Park Pride's 25th annual conference tackled the big question: how do we build park systems that can handle climate change?

One of the most important discussions centered on urban heat islands, which hit Atlanta's underserved communities the hardest. While we like to call ourselves "the city in a forest," that nickname doesn't apply equally everywhere. Some neighborhoods are experiencing dangerous temperature differences because they lack the tree canopy that keeps other parts of town cooler.

This isn't just academic. We're talking about life-and-death issues, since extreme heat is the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the U.S. The conference brought together people working on everything from community tree planting to policy changes that could help communities build coalitions and push for more equitable green space distribution.
stories.imageAlt: Attendees at the Parks & Greenspace Conference at Atlanta Botanical Garden

stories.headline: Historic Documents Land at Atlanta History Center via 'Freedom Plane'
stories.summary: The National Archives' touring "Freedom Plane" brought founding documents to Hartsfield-Jackson, including an 1823 engraving of the Declaration of Independence and a Senate markup of the Bill of Rights. The documents will be on display at the Atlanta History Center through April 12 with free admission, but you need to register online first.
stories.body:
History buffs, this is your moment. The National Archives just flew some incredible founding documents into Hartsfield-Jackson via their "Freedom Plane" tour, and they're setting up shop at the Atlanta History Center for the next few weeks. We're talking about an 1823 engraving of the Declaration of Independence and an actual U.S. Senate markup of the Bill of Rights, among other treasures.

The timing is perfect since we're gearing up for America's 250th birthday celebrations, and Atlanta is one of only eight cities getting this special exhibition. The fact that they landed at the world's busiest airport and then set up at our local history center shows how much national attention Atlanta gets for this kind of cultural programming.

Here's the catch: admission is free, but you absolutely have to register online ahead of time. They've already got 12,000 people signed up, and if you want the best experience, weekdays are your best bet for avoiding crowds. The exhibition runs through April 12, which gives you plenty of time to plan a visit, but don't wait too long to register.
stories.imageAlt: Historic documents from the National Archives Freedom Plane exhibition
